data.title: Snowflake: Don't Be Fooled By Non-GAAP Metrics
data.description:
This is the last image in the Snowflake (SNOW) presentation.

If, like me, you are a fundamental investor, you will certainly be tempted to invest in this growth story. It is basically everything an analyst wants to see in a company's operations. Customers are growing rapidly and are already at a level that gives them consolidated status, the net revenue retention rate is over 120% (showing strong customer loyalty), and revenue is growing 30% YoY, not to mention the very compelling industry in which Snowflake is.

The problem is that my enthusiasm for Snowflake only went so far. Looking at the financials, the company is not yet as mature as it appears, with GAAP margins still under pressure (even though non-GAAP margins are already healthy) due to significant shareholder dilution. Therefore, even with interesting potential for this market, my rating for SNOW is a sell.

There Are Positives in the SNOW Case

Before moving on to the more negative side of the SNOW case, it is worth highlighting some of the company's positives. As mentioned, the highlights are truly impressive, and some points, such as strong revenue growth and many large customers, already show that Snowflake has indeed managed to create a product that users like, remain loyal to the ecosystem, and even increase their contracts. For instance, the company already has 751 customers from the Forbes Global 2000 list.

Another very interesting point is its market, which is closely related to data, AI, and machine learning. According to Snowflake, we are talking about a total addressable market of $170 billion in 2024, with estimates to grow to $355 billion in 2029. If we believe that the company can capture just 10% of this market, we are talking about revenue of $35 billion, much higher than the current revenue of ~$4.6 billion and much higher than market estimates of $10 billion for fiscal year 2030.

For these 2 reasons, the prospects for Snowflake's financials are, in a way, positive. The market sees topline compounding at a rate of around ~25% per year in the coming years, which seems to me to be a very feasible model. With the gains in operating leverage, along with efficiency and potentially lower dilution, the consensus for EPS is already much better at around 40% per year growth.

Snowflake: Valuation is Not Good

Well, if EPS has the potential to grow 40% per year over the next 3 fiscal years, what is the problem with the valuation? It's that the market already prices this in. In fiscal year 2028, growing at this rate, the P/E would still be 101x, as the table below shows.

Even if we say that the company will continue to grow at this rate after that, it will still take years for the shareholder yield to be minimally attractive. In fiscal year 2031, EPS will not even be $6, and today SNOW stock is trading for over $240.

Some listed peers from related industries, such as MongoDB (MDB) and Cloudflare (NET), are also trading at very high multiples, even similar to Snowflake, and deliver similar growth, which makes Snowflake seem not so out of place at first glance.

But we must remember that companies such as Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), and Microsoft (MSFT) also manage to exploit products similar to Snowflake's and still pose a competitive risk. All these 3 companies are infinitely cheaper than Snowflake, although they also have much more diversified businesses and are less exposed to this growth.

SBC is Very Misleading for SNOW

Stock-based compensation (SBC) is crucial to understanding why the valuation is even worse than it seems. The P/E ratios of 100x+ are based on non-GAAP EPS, and when we look at GAAP, it's much worse.

I understand that cash flow is not affected by this expense, as we can see below, the net loss for the first 6 months of 2025 was -$727 million, but after adding mainly depreciation (~$100 million) and SBC (~$780 million), which are somewhat recurring, there is a positive cash flow from operating activities of over $300 million. Even though this is not much for a company with a market cap of $70 billion+, it is a way of showing that cash flow is healthy.

However, in my opinion, this is very misleading. Shareholders are being diluted, and it is not as if this SBC is just a non-cash expense that will occur only once. It is a way of compensating employees. So at first glance, the SNOW’s operating margin, which is 11% non-GAAP, seems to be doing well and improving (since in Q2 2024 it was 5%), but the GAAP operating margin is -30%, much better than the -41% delivered last year, but still a very negative margin. The company will have to scale this revenue significantly until the shareholder value is really interesting and compensates for all this dilution.

Risks for the Bear Case

The two main risks for Snowflake's case are dilution and valuation. But for the bear case, there are also some risks.

The main one is economies of scale. If the company continues to grow and surprise the market (which will require very strong execution), we may see revenue growing at a much faster pace than expenses such as SBC, and consequently GAAP net income would improve, as would valuation.

I don't necessarily hope that SNOW stock will perform poorly, especially since I don't have a short position. But I believe that a correction is quite possible in this scenario, and the risk that the correction will not happen is precisely that the market mood will remain very optimistic about the case, pricing SNOW as a quality compounder for the coming decades and consequently maintaining this premium valuation.

Q3 Earnings and the Bottom Line

Note that Snowflake's Q3 will be next week, and the market expects an EPS of $0.31. While a beat here is important and could spark optimism about the stock, I still prefer to look at other metrics.

Top-line growth will be extremely important because, for the future prospects of a better valuation to be possible, there needs to be continued compounding on the top line, which is precisely why management needs to continue to show that operations are running smoothly and prospects are good.

Finally, at the current level, I would like to start seeing greater concern with SBC and shareholder dilution, showing that the increase in revenue is helping to dilute costs and expenses, including SBC.

In short, I still think Snowflake is a good company; its operations are practically flawless, its prospects are very strong, and its financials are also quite good to a certain extent, but the valuation is not at all compatible with a company that will still take years and years to reach a reasonable non-GAAP P/E, let alone a cheap GAAP multiple (it could take more than a decade, even with compounding).

Therefore, I am initiating my coverage of SNOW with a sell rating.

data.title: Hartford Dynamic Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

US fixed-income markets produced positive total returns in the third quarter of 2025, as political uncertainty and fiscal sustainability concerns prevailed. The US reached several major trade agreements—including with the EU, UK, and Japan—while its trade truce with China was extended for 90 days. US data integrity came under scrutiny after the dismissal of the Bureau of Labor Statistics commissioner and after the institutional credibility concerns surfaced following the attempted removal of Fed Governor Lisa Cook, ultimately blocked by the Supreme Court. Credit spreads 1 tightened further and most fixed-income sectors outperformed US Treasuries.

Performance Summary

  • Hartford Dynamic Bond Fund (I Share) generated positive total returns during the quarter, driven by lower yields and tighter spreads. Treasury positioning contributed to performance. Yields declined in the US as the Federal Reserve (Fed) delivered its first rate cut of 2025, indicating a shift in focus from inflation to labor-market risks.
  • Positioning within credit across investment-grade (IG) credit, high-yield credit, and emerging markets (EM) all contributed to returns. Credit spreads tightened across the rating spectrum and sectors for most corporate bonds. Within IG credit, the energy sector was the primary contributor to returns. Our exposure to utilities issuers was also favorable. Within high-yield credit, communications exposure was one of the primary drivers of returns. Emerging-markets positioning was also additive, led by government-related issuers.

Positioning & Outlook

  • The Fund remains selective and positioned with low levels of risk relative to history in US high yield and EM high yield, where valuations appear compressed when accounting for forward-looking total-return potential.
  • We are keeping duration 2 higher relative to the portfolio's history to reflect better US Treasury prices and a higher probability of an easing Fed. The market has temporarily dismissed US fiscal-deficit worries. With high Treasury yields, historically tight credit spreads, and possible labor-market risks, we favor Treasuries. We believe the intermediate segment of the IG credit market represents the most attractive opportunity. Intermediate IG credit, currently our largest allocation, provides a combination of duration, credit quality, risk, and total return across diverse scenarios.
  • Macroeconomic conditions are uncertain, and ongoing geopolitical risks and fiscal deficits pose a headwind, but AI and inflation may provide a tailwind for fixed income. We're watching for AI-driven effects on growth, productivity, and the US labor market, but we anticipate this will be gradual. We believe growth could slow enough for Fed rate cuts without significantly harming corporate earnings in the short term. Over the medium term, risks increase for a sharper labor-market slowdown as tariffs, immigration policies, and AI impact the economy.

data.title: Epsilon Energy: Newly Acquired Powder River Basin Assets Meaningfully Increases Leverage
data.description:
Intro

Epsilon Energy Ltd. (EPSN) is an onshore oil and gas company operating in Oklahoma, Pennsylvania, New Mexico, and Texas. We wrote about it back in March of this year, when we decided to take a cautious stance as opposed to our bullish recommendation in the previous year. Our downgrade to a 'Hold' mainly stemmed from the company's overhanging valuation (specifically the price of Epsilon's earnings) at the time. The downgrade ended up being the right call, as shares have lost approximately 34% of their value over the past eight months. In fact, as we consult EPSN's intermediate chart, we see that the breakout above the 2022 highs only lasted temporarily before turning over (the Stock topped out in June) into a sustained pattern of lower highs and lower lows. Epsilon stock has come up against some downside support in recent sessions, which has stabilized matters somewhat for the time being. Considering Epsilon's mere $127 million market cap, however, elevated near-term risk remains that we may see lower prices, which recent trends seem to be pointing to.

Due to the magnitude of the downswing over the past five months, Epsilon stock has become heavily oversold, as confirmed by the ultra-sold nature of the intermediate MACD. However, the death-cross (crossing of the 10-week moving average beneath its 40-week counterpart), which took place earlier this, trumps any other technical indicator as it establishes the trend in motion. Therefore, we may indeed have more downside here, especially if the $4.25 to $4.40 fails to provide support over the near term.

The strength of the downtrend in recent months is also a factor, as demonstrated by the stock's rising ADX trend-following indicator. Since established trends tend to persist rather than reverse, the probability is that lower lows or consolidation, at best, is on the cards for Epsilon over the near term.

Remember, the technical charts always instantly let us know how the market (investors) views the company in question. Therefore, whilst Epsilon continues to have serious potential from a leveraging standpoint with respect to the assets it has on its books, searing earnings growth (which would consequently spike the share price) isn't on investors' radar at present, all things remaining equal.

Q3 Earnings

Epsilon reported its third-quarter earnings for the present fiscal year, where GAAP earnings of $0.05 per share came in on reported revenues of $8.98 million (23% year-over-year gain). The CEO outlined the success of the Permian asset to date, noting that drilling and completion of the eighth well proceeded in the quarter. Epsilon's Permian assets continue to give the company oxygen with respect to its cash flow, noting that 40%+ of operating cash flow has already been reaped from the company's spend to date in this asset.

Furthermore, it shouldn't be understated how Epsilon's Q3 revenues grew by over 23%, considering curtailments (concerning Nat-Gas related production) in the Marcellus continue to abound. These types of trends speak to the strength of Epsilon's balance sheet in that (in a commodity-driven environment), it can carry a significant amount of non-producing assets on its books, yet has the capacity to continue to invest in the company aggressively, growing top-line revenues as a result.

Peak Companies Acquisition

What overshadowed the earnings release was updates on the Peak Companies acquisition, which was outlined in August of this year, relating to operated assets in the Powder River Basin. Official releases concerning the closing of this deal (with respect to the issuance of shares, etc) came out later in the month, and it's good to see the share price holding its own, post the closing of the acquisition. In fact, the CFO pointed out that if one were to study the assets which have come onto Epsilon's books and correlated this with Epsilon's share-price action since negotiations commenced, shares are actually cheaper now (from an asset standpoint), despite lower lows in recent months.

One thing to mention on the acquisition, the stock price movement since we first negotiated the deal has worked in our favour from a valuation perspective on the acquired assets. The deal is for a set number of shares to be issued at closing, plus the assumption of debt. Using, for example, $5 per share for Epsilon common, we are acquiring core undeveloped net acreage in the PRB at less than $900 per acre or, thought of another way, paying less than $300,000 per priority location. Both of those metrics, we believe to be discounts to market value.

Significant Operating Leverage Now At Epsilon's Disposal

Notwithstanding the optimization work Epsilon can do on producing wells in the Powder River Basin, the undeveloped inventory in this deal is vast, which is excellent from a financial standpoint, concerning cost reductions that can accrue. Furthermore, as mentioned, with the price of Natural Gas at levels aligned to 2009, we believe it is only a matter of time before this commodity breaks out of its multi-year-long trading range. Once curtailments lift in the Marcellus, we expect to see significant cash-flow gains over time, with the Auburn area having been earmarked for development. Furthermore, the COO laid out how continued Permian strength is expected in 2026, with ongoing discussions regarding an operation for Epsilon's Canadian JV.

Our Permian Basin Barnett project continues to be a solid performer. The eighth well in the play is performing very consistently compared with the first 7 wells. We now have 2 net wells making approximately 575 barrels of oil equivalent per day in the project. At least 2 more Barnett wells, 0.5 net, are planned for 2026. In our Canadian JV, we are in discussions with the operator on potential plans for the next 18 months. And lastly, the company is in the early stages of exploring a sale of our non-core Mid-Con assets in Oklahoma.

Conclusion

Therefore, to sum up, although Epsilon's asset quantity & quality continue to improve, we must remember that the success of these assets is primarily commodity-driven, all things remaining equal. In saying this, management is confident that cash flows will continue to cover the dividend, which presently comes in at 5.4%. Dividend affordability and ongoing hedging limits provide downside protection, enabling investors to hold their positions until trading conditions become more favorable. Maintaining 'Hold' rating. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Gold Price Up 2.5% For The Week. Is A Break Of $4200/Oz A Certainty?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Gold prices are currently maintaining a robust uptrend and trading near two-week highs, around the $4150/oz handle. The precious metal is poised to log its fourth consecutive monthly gain, building on a record-setting surge experienced in October that briefly targeted the $4,400 area.

The continuation of the bullish rally could be a sign that the current rally still has deep support. The bullish trend is reinforced by technical indicators that confirm strong underlying momentum, following a selloff that allowed some profit taking.

Foundational Drivers: The Federal Reserve Pivot Proxy

The current rally and supporting gold's valuation is the aggressive market expectation of impending monetary policy easing by the US Federal Reserve. Current forecasts indicate an 84% probability of a US interest rate cut in December, with broader market consensus pricing in approximately 90 basis points of easing by the end of 2026.

Market participants are expecting the Fed to start cutting rates despite mixed rhetoric. The expectations do keep the US dollar (USD) under broad pressure and force US Treasury yields to drift lower. Gold is a beneficiary in this environment as declining real yields reduce the opportunity cost of holding the metal.

These easing expectations are validated by a sequence of disappointing US economic releases, including softer ADP employment numbers, weaker retail sales figures, and a notable drop in the Conference Board’s Consumer Confidence reading for November. Market participants are continuing a recent trend by actively front-running the central bank’s mandated reaction function.

Technical Outlook - Gold (XAU/USD)

Looking at the four-hour chart below, the technical picture is strong. Price action looks favorable with the RSI above 50, a sign of the bullish momentum.

Price continues to trade some way away from both the 50 and 100-day MA. This could lead to a short-term pullback at some stage.

Given the Thanksgiving holiday in the US tomorrow, markets could see lower levels of liquidity. This could keep gold prices range-bound as well.

Keep a close watch on Ukraine-Russia developments. Any sign that a deal may be edging close could lead to increased selling pressure on gold and thus push gold prices lower.

Gold (XAU/USD) Four-Hour Chart, November 26, 2025

Gold: Key Near-Term Technical Levels

As mentioned above, bulls may struggle to break beyond the $4200/ounce heading into the weekend as liquidity is expected to be thin. This is not to say it is not a possibility.

Key levels to pay attention to include

  • $4,380: All-Time High/Previous Record High (October 17)
  • $4,220: Critical Breakout Resistance Key threshold for acceleration
  • $4042: Key to Uptrend Integrity

data.title: BLOK: High-Tech, High-Growth, Less Heartburn
data.description:
There are many alternatives when it comes to investing in the crypto ecosystem, looking beyond just bitcoins and bitcoin mining companies. I recently wrote about an AI-powered Schwab Crypto Thematic ETF (STCE), and found it a decent Buy. The Amplify Blockchain Technology ETF (BLOK) is another name I recommend. It has been an outperformer, remaining with the top half of the crypto pack (say STCE, DAPP, BKCH) in most market conditions and has some structural advantages (that we will discuss in this article) that make it a long term Buy.

The volatility of the crypto space is huge and investors need to have high growth high risk appetite to be able to ride this ETF. Also, to cut idiosyncratic risks, I particularly emphasize a basket of ETFs each having their own strengths. So, while STCE does find a place in that portfolio for its unbiased rules-based but active-like positioning (backed by performance data), BLOK's active strategy presents a decent balance of investments beyond mining and bitcoin prices, optional plays in AI and a well-diversified portfolio (low single-stock risks).

Methodology and Portfolio

BLOK's investment universe starts from companies meaningfully involved in blockchain development, usage, R&D or commercialization. Managers check for blockchain relevance (including profiting from blockchain based data). The portfolio of 40-60 stocks comprises a 70% core holding in companies who derive significant revenue from blockchain. The remaining portfolio (secondary ~30%) consists of companies having partnerships and investments in the space. Stocks are weighted equally within each group, although the active methodology means deviations over time (particularly given the portfolio size and idiosyncratic volatile moves between stocks). However, individual stock risk looks relatively muted compared to STCE (top weight ~11.3%), VanEck Digital Transformation ETF (DAPP) (top weight ~8.64%) and Global X Blockchain ETF (BKCH) (~13.12%). The top 10 holdings account for a reasonable ~35% of the total portfolio — not too concentrated. For comparison here are the top 10 total weightings: for DAPP ~60%, BKCH ~71% and STCE~60%. The diversification is a real structural strength for a crypto-themed ETF and should help protect the portfolio from adverse movements in single stocks.

Inferences from the Portfolio

Based on a manual scan of the current holdings, I found ~22% investments in bitcoin miners and infrastructure, names like HUT 8 Corp (HUT), Cipher Mining, Inc. (CIFR) and TeraWulf, Inc. (WULF). This is the most correlated part of BLOK to crypto prices and is reasonably low exposure to that extent. Adding another ~8% exposure in bitcoin ETFs and Trusts like iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) or even Strategy Inc (MSTR), the total bitcoin correlated part of the portfolio adds up to only ~30%. The bulk of the portfolio benefits beyond simply bitcoin price moves, unlike a mining focused ETF like say CoinShares Bitcoin Mining ETF (WGMI).

Exchanges and Brokerages account for a significant ~24% of the portfolio — companies like Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD), Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN) and CME Group, Inc. (CME). These companies generate revenues from trading and services fees. They need transaction volumes and not bitcoin price appreciation directly. They can benefit from greater bitcoin adoption and trading, so lower volatility over time in crypto prices is more important than a bull market alone (of course, a bull market helps).

Then there is a ~17% exposure in payments and fintech to companies like PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL), NU Holdings Ltd. (NU) and even MercadoLibre, Inc. (MELI). This layer is very diversified on its own with companies like MercadoLibre's share prices having very low dependency on bitcoin prices. Even the more dependent names like Paypal and Nu Holdings will benefit from increased crypto adoption (i.e. if bitcoin becomes a more accepted currency globally) versus sheer price appreciation.

BLOK also has ~15% in enterprise blockchain and AI-adjacent large caps — NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) and International Business Machines Corporation (IBM) are in this mix. They walk into the ETF on the basis of infrastructure support to mining and blockchain technology (from chips to cloud support). This is a very good diversification away from crypto prices and happens to have an AI optionality too in a lot of cases (like Nvidia and AMD).

Performance Insights

The diversification by theme and a breakaway from the crypto prices provide substantial alpha avenues for BLOK. BLOK could grow in many ways: AI compute, semis, fintech volumes, enterprise software spending or even tokenization. The bulk of BLOK doesn’t need Bitcoin prices to rise - it only needs Bitcoin to keep getting adopted. Adoption happens when volatility falls, not when prices spike alone.

Even within the ~30% of the portfolio more directly linked with bitcoin prices, surprises happen. Some traditional miners are turning into AI compute and hosting, like TeraWulf and Core Scientific, Inc. (CORZ). These shares actually led to a significant surge in BLOK at a time when bitcoin prices did not rise at all. At some point in October, BLOK was up 50% while bitcoin was still flat.

This AI side story was of course very well captured in a bitcoin mining focused ETF like WGMI where IREN Limited (IREN) and Cipher Mining (both together account for over a third of the overall portfolio). WGMI tripled in October due to this evolving story, but has also fallen by 42% since. WGMI's volatility is actually what makes BLOK's diversification more risk aware, both in terms of single stock exposure as well as exposure to several agnostic themes.

I have already charted the outperformance in BLOK versus IBIT (a proxy for bitcoin), illustrating its reward potential versus bitcoins. This comes at the cost of volatility that is lower than bitcoin's. In terms of standard deviations, BLOK shows 10% lower standard deviation than IBIT and less than half of WGMI. Both empirically, and in terms of how the portfolio is structured, expect BLOK to have a far superior risk-reward asymmetry than IBIT or WGMI like bitcoin focused bets.

A comparison with other crypto-themed ETFs also show BLOK has been in the top 2 most of the time over the past 3 years (observe the trajectory and not only the end point outperformance). Here are the standard deviation numbers for the risk/volatility perspective - BLOK ~45, STCE ~63, BKCH ~77 and DAPP ~74. Overall, BLOK is consistently towards the higher end of the total return spectrum within the crypto pack, while its risks are far lower. That calls for overweighing BLOK in any crypto and blockchain portfolio.

Overweight BLOK

I rate BLOK a Buy backed by several empirical evidences and structural advantages. First, its single stock diversification is best-in-class. Secondly, thematic diversification means lower volatility. Third, empirical performance shows these expectations actually translate to real life performance. BLOK outperforms most of the crypto pack most of the time and keeps volatility levels low.

Overall, I am bullish this ETF segment, that invests on a crypto ecosystem rather than just bitcoins and mining. This positions it to gain from simply better adoption over bitcoin appreciation. With regulatory supports coming in, in the US as well as elsewhere, as bitcoin adoption grows, this theme will likely keep growing. Crypto investing cannot be defensive given its volatility, so investors should have the risk appetite to wade through 50% MTM losses at some point. A systematic accumulation over time works. At least the wider crypto plays makes for lower volatility levels - but still may require similar levels of risk appetite. The overall crypto ecosystem bet looks bullish otherwise, with BLOK a material overweight in that portfolio.

data.title: Macquarie Science And Technology Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

The third quarter of 2025 saw continued outperformance from technology stocks as the artificial intelligence (AI) secular growth theme was boosted by earnings and accelerated announcements of AI infrastructure buildout projects. Tech stocks returned 13.1% (as reflected by the S&P North American Technology Sector Index) during 3Q versus 8.1% for the S&P 500 Index.

Below the headline number, semiconductors, semi cap equipment, and hardware outperformed - buoyed by earnings. Memory was a particularly strong group as supply-demand dynamics resulted in several firms increasing prices. In contrast, the debate continues regarding software, an area that we have mostly avoided, where incumbents, including Salesforce (CRM) and Adobe (ADBE), saw challenging returns. Elsewhere, we saw momentum in sports betting, media, and social media break down amid threats of regulation and growing competition.

Source: Bloomberg, unless otherwise noted.

Within the Fund

For 3Q25, Macquarie Science and Technology Fund (WSTAX) Institutional Class (ISTIX) shares had a strong return. Although the Fund underperformed its benchmark, the S&P North American Technology Sector Index, it outperformed the S&P 500 Index. During the quarter, an underweight to software and services along with an overweight to semiconductors and semi cap equipment contributed to relative performance. While underweight tech hardware, our stock selection within the space added value, with portfolio holdings Seagate Technology Holdings PLC (STX) and Arista Networks Inc. (ANET) outperforming. In contrast, holdings in media and entertainment as well as select consumer and healthcare stocks detracted, failing to keep pace with tech gains.

Among individual stocks, Seagate, Unity Software Inc. (U) , and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. Ltd. (TSM) (TSMC) were the strongest relative performers.

  • Seagate, a data center storage provider, outperformed given the surge in storage requirements from the increased content created from AI applications, including video files. We continue to believe that we are at an inflection in computing will drive exponential growth in storage demand.
  • Unity Software, a leading provider of real-time development platform used for creating games and interactive experiences, outperformed, buoyed by a new product cycle (Vector), which already started to translate into improved performance in customer acquisition for developers, and more significant benefits expected in 2026.
  • TSMC, the world's leading semiconductor manufacturer, rose amid robust demand for chips due to rising computing intensity of models with growing usage and reasoning capabilities.

Detractors from performance during the quarter included SAP SE (SAP) , Monday.com Ltd. , and an underweight to Apple Inc. (AAPL) which outperformed.

  • SAP, the German enterprise software maker, declined amid broader software sector weakness despite strong underlying business growth. The outlook for SAP remains positive in our view, with continued product upgrade cycle augmented by AI-related opportunities. Given the prior valuation re-rating, we moderated our exposure during the period.
  • Monday.com, a cloud-based work operating system, underperformed amid disappointing 2Q earnings results, with investors' concerns around moderating growth heightened by anxiety over AI's potential to commoditize customer relationship and project management tools. We maintain a small position in light of the risk/reward on the stock.
  • An underweight to Apple detracted as shares reversed much of the earlier year-to-date declines in 3Q. The rebound was fueled by renewed investor enthusiasm around Apple's AI potential ("Apple Intelligence") along with early momentum for the iPhone 17 launch, which emphasized AI features including enhanced Siri and on-device processing. We remain skeptical about Apple's AI strategy and view Apple as lagging in generative AI. In the short term, Apple may benefit from this new product cycle, its medium growth profile is mature in our view.

Outlook: A once-in-a-generation data center buildout opportunity

AI infrastructural buildout accelerates:

The recent news flow shows no sign of slowing for the AI physical infrastructure layer (which we had previously referred to as Stage 1 of this new tech wave). Governments are fast tracking approvals and hyperscalers (and neo-hyperscalers) are raising billions to build data center capacity to answer ever-growing computing intensity. To exemplify this point, let's look at the US-based Stargate project, a partnership involving OpenAI, Oracle (ORCL), and Softbank (OTCPK:SFTBY), which is of extraordinary scale. To give some perspective, OpenAI expects it needs 900,000 DRAM wafers per month to operate its advanced AI models. This is equivalent to more than 100% of current revenues for the three main memory semiconductor firms. Such extraordinary investments are naturally raising concerns. To be clear, we see no supply glut on the horizon.

Exponential computational need:

For once, large language models (LLM) are becoming very capable, with the ability to “think” – referred to as “reasoning models.” This is a significant advancement from just a few months ago, where models generated outputs based on memorization (“training”). Today, models “think” and, while thinking, models do research and learn more about a topic before providing answers. This process has led to exponential computational requirements to the order of thousands if not millions of times more computationally intensive. Alongside models getting “more capable,” demand and use cases increase. The adoption of generative AI has also increased significantly. According to the Ramp Economics Lab, the share of US businesses with paid subscriptions to AI models, platforms, and tools rose to 44.5% (Their sample includes more than 40,000 American businesses and billions of dollars in corporate spending using data from Ramp’s corporate card and bill pay platform). The pace of monetization is not yet on par with the capex requirements, but that is something we expect in the future with new applications and products. Notwithstanding this, it bears watching.

Potential for new applications and products

Beyond LLMs, we are witnessing a rapid pace of innovation at the application layer. These new AI applications are able to replace a number of incumbents’ software (if they fail to integrate AI tools effectively) while AI agents (of “digital humans”) may be able to replicate the output of workers at a fraction of the cost, with the potential to transform workflows across industries. In our view, existing moats of incumbent tech companies (and beyond tech) could be tested in the next few quarters. But it’s not the just in the application layer that volatility should be expected. The underpinning of the semiconductor trade could also evolve. Hyperscalers are increasingly looking to cut reliance on Nvidia by designing their own chips in search of greater efficiency and affordability.

And we are just scratching the surface of the possibilities. In our framework, Stage 3 will be about AI disruption, the creation of new products and industries, and, while still unknown, it will require all of us to be nimble and adaptive as companies that are currently building and monetizing may not be the ones doing so in the future.

Conclusion and positioning

In our view, we are at a critical point of this multi-year AI cycle, which should benefit active management as we look to define the future winners and losers of this new tech wave. At present, we continue to favor AI enablers, including the semi and hardware firms that we have emphasized in the portfolio over the last 2+ years while looking opportunistically at software, healthcare and other industries that are embracing AI. The underlying forces of innovation should continue to power technology stocks over the next several years, and we will continue to use volatility to position the portfolio to participate in the next stage of growth.

data.title: Nio: Another Disappointing Quarterly Report
data.description:
Tuesday morning, we received third quarter results from Nio (NIO). The Chinese electric vehicle maker is in the midst of a major growth boom, having expanded its offerings to sell more affordable vehicles over the last year or so. While the company is doing well to improve margins and cut its overall losses, management again disappointed investors on the revenue front and its current quarter delivery forecast.

Previous coverage of the name

My most recent article on Nio came back in early September, right after the company had reported its Q2 results. I discussed how things were progressing in the right direction, but a little slower than I would have liked. Guidance for Q3 was very weak, but management was calling for major growth towards the end of 2025. Since that article, Nio shares have lost more than 7.5%, while the S&P 500 is up over 4%.

A look at the Q3 results

For the September period, Nio delivered a little over 87,000 vehicles. That was somewhat disappointing, even though it was more than 40% growth, as it was barely above the low end of the guidance range, a forecast that was given well into that quarter. Now that Nio has moved downmarket with its Onvo and Firefly brands, unit growth has definitely picked up.

With average selling prices coming down, vehicle revenues increased by 15% over the Q3 2024 figure. Total company revenues showed 16.7% growth to $3.06 billion, but the street was expecting almost 22% growth to $3.14 billion, even after a $300 million average estimate cut in recent months. This is the fifth straight quarter where Nio has missed street revenue estimates and the ninth time in the past twelve quarters.

As the company's production ramps have progressed, margins have started to rise a bit. Vehicle margins were up 160 basis points over the prior year period, with the company's total gross margin up twice that amount. The company was able to reduce its adjusted operating loss by nearly 40% and its adjusted net loss by a similar amount. However, that still was a comprehensive loss of more than half a billion dollars for the quarter.

Unfortunately for investors, current quarter guidance was another massive disappointment. Management is looking for deliveries to be in a range of 120,000 to 125,000 for Q4, well below the 150,000 target that they had previously discussed on the Q2 conference call. As a result of reduced deliveries, it's not a surprise that even the high end of the revenue forecast for $4.60 to $4.78 billion came in well below the street's average analyst estimate of $4.90 billion.

Analysts had raised their Q4 numbers quite meaningfully after that 150,000 target was discussed previously, but now they'll have to take them back down a bit. On the conference call, management cited policy changes regarding trade-ins and subsidies for the shortfall, saying that it prevents the normal end of year sales surge. However, the purchase tax benefit is being halved, so that should have provided somewhat of a benefit for Nio.

Balance sheet update

I mentioned in my previous article that Nio's working capital had gone quite a bit negative, potentially meaning a capital raise was coming. That happened later in September, with the company bringing in $1.16 billion in new funding by selling almost 210 million shares. That improved the working capital picture as seen in the chart below, although the number still remains a bit in the red.

Management detailed a $5.1 billion cash position at the end of Q3 - which is a combination of cash, restricted cash, short-term investments, and long term time deposits at the end of September, up about $1.3 billion for the three month period. Total borrowings remained at a little more than $2 billion, with the capital raise helping drive the cash increase, but this doesn't include more than $5.5 billion in trade and notes payable. The share count used to calculate earnings per share was almost 2.43 billion for the September 2025 period, up meaningfully from the nearly 2.06 billion seen a year earlier.

The current valuation picture

Since many of the Chinese electric vehicle names are not profitable, even on a non-GAAP basis, the best valuation metric currently is price-to-sales. In the chart below, you can see how NIO compares to BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) (OTCPK:BYDDY), XPeng (XPEV), Li Auto (LI), and ZEEKR (ZK) on a price to expected revenue basis for next year. At 0.77 times projected 2026 sales as of Tuesday afternoon, NIO traded right at the average of the other four names. However, this was before factoring in any potential revenue estimate changes, which might come down a little given the Q3 miss and weak Q4 guidance.

As for street analysts, the picture has changed a bit since I last looked at NIO, at which point the average price target called for considerable upside. The average as of Tuesday afternoon was $6.83, calling for more than 20% upside from current levels. In my previous article, analysts were looking for roughly that amount of downside in the stock. However, I'll point out that the street average was in the low $60s until the start of 2022, so analysts have been reducing their NIO valuations over time as the stock has moved lower.

Final thoughts and recommendation

Tuesday's Q3 report was another major disappointment from Nio. The company again missed street revenue estimates, while giving current quarter delivery guidance well below management's target from less than three months ago. While margins are improving, the growth isn't coming as fast as hoped, meaning current quarter analyst revenue estimates will head lower again. The company did improve its balance sheet during the September ending period, but it came via meaningful dilution to shareholders.

Given a fair valuation at current levels, I'm going to stay neutral on Nio shares for the time being. I do like the improving margin picture and reduced losses, but yet again management put out a sky high future growth target only to then lower it when the time came. Perhaps if management was a bit more realistic over time, I could change my rating, but I'm not in favor of buying a name that consistently sets up its investors for disappointment.

data.title: Generation PMCA Q3 2025 Quarterly
data.description:
Long Live Value Investing

Some have argued that value investing is dead. Growth names have considerably outperformed. Passive (index-linked) investments now represent the majority of ETFs and mutual funds. These two phenomena have been reinforcing because the growth companies—pricier companies whose valuations tend to overshoot as growth is prized—represent such a disproportionate portion of major indices. Because information is so readily available, markets are highly efficient and many contend that markets no longer produce meaningful anomalies. As such, strategies focused on value have been regarded as outdated.

But claims of the death of value investing are always prevalent near market tops, when speculation has unduly lifted securities, seemingly without reference to underlying value. Bitcoin is up because bitcoin is up. Gold is way up. What kind of “safe haven” is as volatile as stocks? And it trades about 2.7x its cost of production—a level only briefly reached by 4 other commodities in the last 50 years, all of which promptly sold off dramatically. Many stocks are also ignoring underlying fundamentals. Anything AI related has been raging.

Active managers generally underperform underlying stock indexes. It’s mainly because indexes are designed to include the strongest companies—most are cap weighted, overweighting the largest ones—while weaklings are culled. No fees or commissions in indexes helps too. Over the last 20 years, fewer than 2% of all U.S. mutual funds have managed to beat the S&P 500. As such, why would investors not simply passively invest in index ETFs?

Stacking the Deck in Our Favour

To us, the answer is simple; by employing the tenets of value investing we can buy high-quality undervalued securities that are being ignored, while avoiding poor quality or expensive ones. Therefore, our process begins with specifically designed screens of leading securities and/or markets to distill a list of those that are likely to outperform. Then, these positions are assessed utilizing our TRAC™ tool to optimize entry and exit points.

Some assert that stock prices alwaysreflect the value of companies. But prices fluctuate way too much to constantly be indicative of value. Most academics argue markets are mostly efficient, and we agree; however, inefficiencies certainly exist. Though, in our wired world, inefficiencies don’t last as long. Therefore, we need to react relatively quickly when an opportunity presents itself, and similarly when a position reverts to our estimate of its Fair Market Value (FMV).

Indexes are currently positioned to underperform. Markets have been more efficient than usual leaving fewer companies undervalued. Some have traded too high above their underlying values, pushing overall indexes too high, right to ceilings in our work.

In the short term, market psychology drives prices. Over time, economic reality prevails and prices coincide with values derived from businesses’ bottom-lines. That’s right out of the value investing handbook. Value investing isn’t dead; it’s just been overshadowed.

Weakening Fundamentals

Across the U.S. and Canada, construction is contracting for housing and commercial buildings. You don’t need to tell Toronto condo builders there’s a contraction. Sales activity is 90% below the 10-year average and prices per square foot are down over 22% from their 2022 peak.

Lenders generally have become anxious. For example, lenders are changing terms to require unanimous consent before borrowers can cause a new loan to rank ahead of theirs. At the rate this is occurring—over 80% of all credit deals—it’s possibly an early-warning sign about impeding credit issues. Delinquencies are already up for consumers and small businesses, while sub-prime borrowers are feeling an even greater pinch. Business bankruptcies are running high too.

In the first 3 quarters of 2025, companies cut 55% more jobs than the comparable period last year, the most since the 2020 recession. October’s job cuts were the most for October since 2003. Consumer sentiment is awful and, based on history, is foreshadowing much more unemployment.

Capacity utilization has weakened globally, down to about 75% from its peak of 79%. This likely means additional idle capacity, lower capital spending, increased corporate financial pressures, and potential price cuts. While it doesn’t auger well for economic growth, it implies lower inflation. Disinflationary pressures should also continue from AI—a productivity enhancer and expense reducer—as well as tariffs which inhibit global trade, and other growth suppressors such as high government debt levels, contracting money supply, and anemic population growth. As a result, lower interest rates are likely.

The economy has been buoyed by spending on AI infrastructure which has been astounding, though it is unlikely to last at its current level. U.S. IT spending has risen to nearly 4.5% of GDP, just below where it peaked in the dot-com bubble. Further evidence that it’s overdone, Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), and Meta (META) have spent 10 times what they’re receiving in AI revenue this year, and profits, if any, would be negligible. These companies are now spending over 60% of their cash flow on capital expenditures, up from a more normal average of 30%. And we won’t even delve into the complications around the key players who seem to be funding each other, i.e. investing in/financing customers.

GDP growth rates have already fallen and profits margins nearly always follow too but, unusually, margins have increased for the past year or so.

Competition from China is coming. Though U.S. protectionism could slow its onset, misplaced U.S. policies are forcing lasting structural changes. The political climate has led many Chinese born academics and tech experts to flee the U.S. and return to China. Already, China produces many products more efficiently and much cheaper. Now, with better funding, more R&D, and operating in a more competitive fashion, with more urgency, it could catapult ahead. China is responsible for half the world's most-cited scientific papers, compared to a decline to one-third from the U.S. China could take the lead in key areas such as AI, quantum computing, and biotech.

Priced for Perfection

Yet, major U.S. and Canadian stock markets are close to all-time highs, at or below TRAC™ ceilings, and priced above FMVs, leaving them vulnerable to price declines. Long-term attractiveness is poor too, since real returns (net of inflation) have never been positive over a 10-year period when equity valuations have been this high. The ratio of the Wilshire 5000 (the broadest measure of U.S. corporate prices) to after-tax profits has only been higher during the dot-com bubble.

The stock markets don’t seem to care. Market speculation has continued. Equity call option volume relative to puts—bullish bets versus bearish—is the highest on record. Equity ownership is at all-time highs, itself implying poor 10-year expected returns. And the Russell 2000 small cap companies that are unprofitable were up 30% in 6 weeks through mid-October, while high-quality stocks’ underperformance is at a low not seen since the dot-com bubble.

Given that valuations are so high, U.S. growth is weakening, and our Economic Composite, TEC™, is still alerting us to a potential recession, we continue to maintain hedging strategies by either holding short positions (where authorized) or inverse long ETFs.

Our Model Portfolios

Our managed accounts are invested based on one or more of our Models (particular investment strategies with notional allocations of securities). A managed account’s holdings will generally be similar to its applicable Model’s, but may not hold all of them based on client-specific factors (income requirements, tax-related considerations, requests/restrictions, and cash available for purchases) and/or market forces which impact specific investment decisions from time to time.

The following descriptions of the holdings in our managed accounts are intended only to explain the reasons that we have made, and continue to hold, these investments in the accounts we manage for you and are not intended as advice or recommendations with respect to purchasing, selling or holding the securities described. Below, we discuss each of our new holdings and updates on key holdings if there have been material developments.

All Cap Model

The All Cap Model combines selections from our large cap strategy (Global Insight) with our small and medium cap ideas. We generally prefer large cap companies for their superior liquidity and lower volatility. The smaller cap positions tend to be less liquid and more volatile; however, we may hold these positions where they are cheaper, trading at relatively greater discounts to our FMV estimates, making their risk/reward profiles favourable.

There were no recent material changes in our smaller cap holdings, and no smaller cap purchases or sales. Our large cap positions are summarized in the Global Insight section.

Global Insight (Large Cap) Model

Global Insight portfolios hold large cap stocks (typically with market caps over $5 billion at the time of purchase but may include those in the $2-5 billion range) where portfolios are managed Long/Short or Long only. At an average of less than 70 cents-on-the-dollar versus our FMV estimates, our Global Insight holdings appear much cheaper, in aggregate, than the overall market.

We adjusted our lululemon (LULU) FMV estimate down to $260 to reflect the impact from U.S. tariffs. While we expected some margin erosion from tariffs, we did not expect the removal of the de minimis exemptions (one could receive products in the U.S. with a value of less than $800 without duties). lululemon itself wasn’t expecting that removal, if at all, until 2027. And the impact is widespread since 92% of all cargo shipped to the U.S. (about 4 million packages a day) apparently fell under de minimis exemptions prior to the removal in August. Approximately two-thirds of lululemon’s online U.S. shipments were being fulfilled by its Canadian warehouses. The company already has plans to offset half the impact of the additional tariffs and de minimis exemption removal. Potential price increases could further assist in alleviating these cost pressures. It also blames itself for being too lax with respect to new product launches, so that should be revitalized going forward too. The company is adding about 40 new stores annually, or over 5% per year to its store base. Its return on invested capital remains way above the average company, even with its depressed forward earnings base, which should be a temporary low point.

In the last few months, we have made several changes in our large-cap positions. We bought Adobe (ADBE), Blue Owl Capital (OWL), and Wise. We sold Marvell Technology (MRVL), Uber Technologies (UBER), Universal Health Services (UHS), and Warner Brothers (WBD) after each ran up TRAC™ceilings near our FMV estimate and we sold Alibaba Health Information Technology, Ping An Insurance, and Alibaba Group as each broke below TRAC™ floors.

Adobe provides productivity and creative software for businesses, creators, and marketing professionals. Key products include Acrobat, Express, Firefly, and Creative Cloud. Naysayers have argued that artificial intelligence apps such as Sora and Nano Banana could replace the need for Adobe’s creative suite. We believe Adobe’s products should remain the gold standard for creative work since its suite is the only solution that synthesizes commercially safe AI with powerful tools for human input, options for custom art, video, and music, and final formatting for print, video, web, and social media. We view AI as a growth driver, not an existential threat. Top-line growth should be in the high-single-digits and free cash flow could hit $10 billion this fiscal year. Our FMV estimate is $440.

Blue Owl Capital is a rapidly growing U.S. asset manager with over $250 billion under management. Its funds operate primarily in direct lending with holdings also in various General Partnerships and private REITs. Almost all its fee revenue is from permanent capital, because of multi-year lockups or indefinite holding periods in the case of its listed Business Development Companies or GP stakes. While the stock has fallen because the market has become concerned with private credit risks generally, the company underwrites its loans to Great Recession conditions, the average loan-to-value is only 39%, and historical credit losses have been a mere 11 basis points. Direct lending should continue to capture market share from banks and other traditional lenders. Blue Owl trades well below our estimated FMV of $27 and yields 6.4%.

Wise is a UK-based financial services company whose products have been designed to offer a faster, cheaper, foreign exchange solution for retail and business customers. Where banks often charge 2-3% for cross-border transactions, which are burdened with administration, Wise transfers average only 52 basis points (which it keeps reducing) and over 74% of its transactions are instantaneous. A Wise VISA debit card is a useful tool for any international traveler. Over 70% of new customers have been generated by word of mouth but the company is now marketing aggressively to further advance its already robust growth. Earnings are expected to continue to grow by 15-20% annually. The company should not be susceptible to competition from Stablecoins, which are geared to one currency. Wise aims to minimize competitive threats by increasing the number of countries where it’s registered under regulations to operate, onboarding major partners, and lowering prices. Our FMV estimate is £13.5.

Multifaceted Diversification

We also aim to limit volatility and drawdowns by combining investment strategies, especially where returns are less correlated. The goal is to outperform through economic cycles with low correlation, therefore less susceptibility, to market index declines. We can construct portfolios with multiple unique return drivers—strategies that differ in style and approach—based on bottom-up fundamentals, macro tools, or pure quantitative analysis. This can provide exposure to different styles and asset classes beyond just stock and bond indexes.

The benefits of multifaceted diversification are not only from different ways to perform but also from a portfolio comprised of strategies that are less correlated. So that when a strategy underperforms, it’s less likely to occur at the same time as another strategy, which softens the volatility and drawdowns of the overall investment portfolio.

Global Tactical Allocation Model

Our Global Tactical Allocation Model (GTAM) investment process combines macroeconomic analysis with valuation and momentum. ETFs (exchange traded funds) are used to gain exposure to 4 broad asset classes: Equities—major markets, emerging markets, sectors, styles, private equity; Fixed Income—bonds issued by governments, investment grade corporations, high-yield issuers, as well as mortgages, and bond indexes; Real Assets—real estate, infrastructure, renewable energy; and Commodities—Precious Metals or Oil. GTAM emphasizes ETFs that should outperform based on the macro environment, are selling at attractive absolute and relative valuations, possess good relative price momentum, and are at TRAC™ floors.

Current exposures are: Equities (44%); Fixed Income (31%); Real Assets (23%); and Commodities (none). Its current broad themes are emerging markets, U.S. real estate, U.S. consumer staples, and U.S. healthcare. The consumer staples and healthcare sectors are undervalued and sitting at TRAC™ floors. In fixed income, long-term U.S. bonds were sold after rallying on rate cut anticipation.

Quantitative Investment Models

Quantitative equity strategies commonly select securities based on systematic, rules-based decisions, using technology to uncover and exploit historical statistically significant anomalies. Our quantitative equity strategies employ proprietary and systematic processes that rank large cap stocks based on factors such as relative valuation, operating metrics (quality), financial strength, and price momentum. The two models noted below select approximately 40 holdings from the top-ranked stocks in the model’s respective universe. Portfolios are rebalanced every 6 months with interim partial rebalancing. TRAC™ is also utilized to optimize entry and exit points.

The Quantitative Global Value Model (QGVM) invests in large-cap equities from around the world. The U.S., Canada, and Japan currently represent the top three countries. The top 3 sectors are Energy (19%), Industrials (16%) and Communication Services (9%). QGVM has a price-to-earnings ratio of 15x, much lower than 23.6x for the ACWI. The model’s dividend yield is 3.4% versus the ACWI’s 1.6%.

The Quantitative Canadian Value Model (QCVM) restricts its universe to Canada’s S&P/TSX Composite. The top 3 sectors are currently Materials (25%), Energy (16%), and Industrials (11%). After an incredible multi-year run, most precious metals companies are at or near TRAC™ ceilings; we expect to rotate out of our precious metal positions and use proceeds to invest in more undervalued opportunities. We believe QCVM has similar quality metrics as the S&P/TSX, yet on average, QCVM’s holdings have a lower valuation (median forward P/E of QCVM is 12.4x, versus the S&P/TSX constituents 15.1x) and a higher dividend yield (3.1% compared to 2.3% for the index).

Income Model

Our high-yield investment strategy has an average current annual yield (income we receive as a percent of current market value of income securities held) of about 5.4%, and most of our holdings—corporate bonds/debentures, preferred shares, REITs, and high-yielding common shares—trade below our FMV estimates.

U.S. high-yield corporate bonds (ICE BofA Index) yield 6.8%. Long-term U.S. government bond yields have remained higher than most expect because inflation remains higher than bondholders prefer. High-yield corporate bond spreads are also quite narrow relative to history. We expect a widening, especially if the economy weakens and corporate delinquencies escalate.

We recently purchased Blue Owl Capital (described above under Global Insight) and XIOR Student Housing, Europe’s largest student housing company because we were attracted by its 100% occupancy rate, 5% steady rental growth rate, new projects, discount to our €39 estimated FMV, and 6% dividend yield. We sold Northland Power (NPI:CA) and HA Sustainable Infrastructure (HASI), as both rose to TRAC™ ceilings in line with our FMV estimates.

Investment Grade Income Model

Our investment grade strategy utilizes a systematic process to rank Canadian investment-grade rated corporate bonds based on their duration, yield, financial strength, and momentum.

Currently, positioning has emphasized longer-dated bonds—duration is 10.6 years, 5 years more than the S&P Canada Investment Grade Corporate Bond Index. The average yield-to-maturity is 5.1% versus 4.1% for the index.

A Rare Breed

As indexes run higher and passive investing gains even more converts, value investors are becoming scarce. But bottom-up security selection based on value and quality, while not easy, should be sought out and embraced, especially when market valuations are so high.

Value investing uses common sense. We endeavour to buy at a discount and sell at fair market value. We emphasize quality businesses, diversify holdings, and hedge via shorting or inverse longs when our models indicate a recession or bear market. We can diversify across strategies too, for exposure to additional assets classes or geographies which ought to reduce drawdowns and volatility. None of that sounds like it should be scarce.

The best investment ideas tend to come from well-sourced idea generation. We continue to integrate all our tools to screen for the best new potential investments, to constantly parse and analyze the results, and to find appropriate entry and exit points for those selected for inclusion.

Underlying fundamentals ultimately matter, even though psychology can overwhelm for some time. Just like their investments, value investors themselves are currently ignored. But time and logic are on our side. We’re nowhere near extinction.

Long live value investing.

Randall Abramson, CFA

All investments involve risk, including loss of principal. This document provides information not intended to meet objectives or suitability requirements of any specific individual. This information is provided for educational or discussion purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a solicitation to buy or sell securities. The information contained herein has been drawn from sources which we believe to be reliable; however, its accuracy or completeness is not guaranteed. This report is not to be construed as an offer, solicitation or recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities herein named. We may or may not continue to hold any of the securities mentioned. Generation PMCA Corp., its affiliates and/or their respective officers, directors, employees or shareholders may from time to time acquire, hold or sell securities named in this report. It should not be assumed that any of the securities transactions or holdings discussed were or will prove to be profitable, or that the investment decisions we make in the future will be profitable or will equal the investment performance of the securities discussed herein. E.&O.E.
