data.title: Main Street Capital: What Bubble?
data.description:
Investors love to look at Main Street Capital’s (MAIN) relatively high price-to-book value and immediately declare it an overvalued bubble of a Business Development Company (“BDC”). But what many investors don’t realize is it’s a fundamentally different type of BDC given its steady net asset value (“NAV”) gains (while peers stay relatively flat—or worse). So while some thumb their nose at Main Street’s mere 5.4% dividend yield (puny in the BDC space), they fail to recognize the steady dividend growth, plus the powerful supplemental dividends, as well as the many other attractive qualities that set it apart (i.e. low debt, strong liquidity, above-average pipeline, healthy portfolio, efficient operations and macro resilience). This report reviews all of that (and the big risks) and then concludes with my strong opinion on investing.

Main Street Capital Facts

Main Street Capital Corporation is a BDC providing long-term debt and equity capital to lower middle-market (“LMM”) companies (those with annual revenues between $10 million and $150 million) as well as debt to private equity-backed firms. It invests across diverse industries and its internal management structure (which allows for more efficient operations) makes it special.

Additionally, as a regulated investment company (“RIC”), it distributes at least 90% its income as dividends to avoid corporate taxes, and also thereby enabling those big steady monthly dividends many investors love.

Importantly, Main Street’s focus on the lower middle market provides a differentiated competitive advantage by addressing an underserved, fragmented segment where larger BDCs and institutions often overlook opportunities (and thereby allowing MAIN to secure attractive risk-adjusted returns through its specialized niche expertise).

Main Street Vs. Other BDCs

A lot of investors avoid Main Street because its current dividend yield is relatively low in the BDC space and its price-to-book value (a basic BDC valuation metric) is relatively high (see table below).

Compare MAIN with (ARCC) (OBDC) (BXSL) (FSK) (GBDC) (PSEC) (OCSL) (CSWC) (TRIN).

However, a lot of investors fail to recognize many of the unique and attractive qualities that make these Main Street metrics not only acceptable, but actually quite attractive, as will be reviewed in the paragraphs below.

Growing NAV

Unlike a lot of its peers, Main Street’s business approach has allowed it to steadily grow its net asset value over time (as you can see in the chart below).

And a growing NAV (or “book value”) also allows it to increase the dividend (e.g., a higher asset base upon which to earn income). And encouragingly, the most recent third quarter marked the 13th consecutive quarter of record NAV per share (see table chart above). It also has an impressive 17% annualized ROE (return on equity) which underscores its long-term value creation for investors.

Dividend Health

With both regular dividends (paid monthly) and supplemental dividends, Main Street is a dividend powerhouse. In fact, the board just declared a $0.30 supplemental dividend (payable Dec 2025—it’s 17th consecutive quarter) and raised the Q1 2026 monthly dividend 4% YoY to $0.26/share. And notably, trailing 12-month supplemental dividends total $1.20/share (40% above regular), with expectation for another significant March 2026 supplement tied to excess DNII (distributable net investment income) and realized gains.

Also, Q3 DNII before taxes was $1.07 per share (guidance ≥$1.05 Q4), which supports dividend coverage even if nonrecurring income (recently $4.3M in Q3) moderates.

For a little more perspective, you can see weighted average interest coupon on Main Street’s existing LMM debt investments in the table below (it’s basically well above the actual dividend yield, which demonstrates how they generate the income, but also that they have plenty of cushion built into the business).

Efficient Operations and Cost Structure

As an internally-managed BDC, Main Street has a significant operating cost advantage (see chart below) plus lower perceived conflicts of interest versus externally managed BDCs.

For example, “non-interest operating expenditures” versus average assets is only 1.4% on an annualized basis—among the industry lowest.

Conservative Leverage and Strong Liquidity

As you can see in the earlier BDC comparison table, Main Street has lower debt-to-equity than peers (which basically gives it more cushion if the market gets ugly, or more dry powder for when compelling opportunities arise). Further, “regulatory debt-to-equity” was 0.62x (below 0.8–0.9x target) with $1.5B+ liquidity (post the recent $350M 5.4% notes issuance and $150M early repayment). This puts Main Street in a position of strength (and able to take advantage of pipeline opportunities), especially considering most of its debt is first lien (safer) anyway (as you can see in the chart below).

Above-Average Investment Pipeline

According to Main Street CEO, Dwayne Hyzak, on the most recent quarterly call, the BDC’s pipeline is above average:

“Now turning to our current investment pipeline. As of today, I would characterize our lower middle market investment pipeline as above average. Consistent with our experience in prior periods of broad economic uncertainty, we believe that our ability to provide unique and flexible financing solutions to lower middle market companies and their owners and management teams and our differentiated long-term to permanent holding periods represent an even more attractive solution to the needs of many lower middle market companies given the current economic environment, and we are confident in our expectations for strong lower middle market investment activity in the fourth quarter.”

This encouraging environment (supported by Main Street healthy liquidity and low credit) is also supported by recent LMM portfolio outperformance and exit potential. For example, according to Hyzak (on the most recently quarterly call):

“We also continue to see significant interest from potential buyers in several of our lower middle market portfolio companies, which we expect will lead to favorable realizations over the next few quarters and which we believe further highlights the strength and quality of our portfolio companies and their exceptional leadership teams.”

These are investments with strong dividend income and fair value appreciation from positive performance. Main Street’s pipeline is healthy.

Asset Management Business Momentum

Important to mention, Main Street also has a healthy asset management business. Specifically, Main Street is the investment adviser and administrator to two private funds, each of which is solely focused on MAIN’s Private Loan investment strategy. This has a significantly positive impact on Main Street’s financial results, including $8.8 million contribution to net investment income in the third quarter of 2025 ($25.4 million contribution to net investment income in the nine months ended September 30, 2025, and $34.3 million contribution to net investment income in the year ended December 31, 2024). This is a diversified income stream adding differentiated degree of steadiness to the business.

The Big Risks

Of course, Main Street faces risks that investors should consider.

Non-accruals and default: For starters, Main Street is providing capital to riskier private companies in the lower middle market. The high coupon interest on the loans it provides (as we saw in an earlier graphic) demonstrates the higher risks and is elevated in attempt to compensate for taking on the risk. Nonetheless, default remains a risk, particularly if macroeconomic conditions worsen and multiple companies all default on their loans at the same time. Fortunately, nonaccruals improved to 1.2% of fair value (3.6% cost) in the most recent quarter (see table below), relatively low and somewhat expected.

Credit Spreads are another way (a proxy) to more-broadly measure risk and potential default among riskier loans, such as those made by Main Street. However, as you can see in the chart below, credit spreads remain relatively low.

(Click here for the full market risk report.)

And according to Main Street CFO, Ryan Nelson, (during the most recent quarterly call), the company remains relatively well-positioned in this regard:

“Our total investment income for the third quarter was $139.8 million, increasing by $3 million or 2.2% over the third quarter of 2024 and decreasing by $4.1 million or 2.9% from the second quarter of 2025. Interest income decreased by $7.3 million from a year ago and increased by $2.4 million from the second quarter of 2025. The decrease from prior year was principally attributable to a decrease in interest rates, primarily resulting from decreases in benchmark index rates on our floating rate debt investments and decreases in interest rate spreads on existing debt investments and an increase in investments on nonaccrual status, partially offset by the impact of increased net investment activity.”

Interest Rates: Interest rate dynamics also pose a risk, as you can see in the table below. However, Main Street’s strong liquidity and lower leverage add a margin of safety against changing rates.

The Bottom Line

Despite the relatively lower yield (as compared to other BDCs) and the relatively higher price-to-book value (as compared to other BDCs), Main Street Capital is not an overvalued bubble. Quite the contrary, it’s a top-notch, differentiated BDC, offering a powerful growth trajectory in dividend and net asset value. Not only have Main Street’s investors easily outperformed other BDCs over the last nearly 20 years since its IPO (see earlier chart), but I expect Main Street to continue outperforming in the years ahead. If you are an income-focused investor, it’s absolutely worth considering for a spot in your prudently diversified, long-term portfolio. I own it in mine.

data.title: Fiserv: The Guidance Cut Masks A Turnaround Opportunity
data.description:
Investment thesis

Fiserv (FISV) is a global payment and fintech company that has reset its expectations in the guidance provided in the Q3 2025 earnings release. The market has penalized the stock in a pretty aggressive way (Figure 1), lowering the stock price from $126.17 per share to the current $60.84. The market anticipated problems with the company, and the stock traded at $235.69 in March 2025. I consider this dip excessive, and my valuation concludes the company is worth at least $112.1 per share, an 84.4% premium over its stock price. With conservative assumptions, I value management’s audacity to change the direction of the company to focus more on the customer and platform development. I appreciate the current position of Fiserv in the market and the Clover platform as essential assets for a turnaround.

Execution risks are relevant in a market with pressure from emerging software companies that remains quite competitive, and a successful turnaround is not assured. Even with those considerations, my estimation of the probability of a value higher than the current stock price is 85.75%.

Shifting expectations

Guidance has been lowered from a revenue growth rate of 10% to 12% to a range of 3.5% to 4.0% in organic revenue growth. The reasons for the right-sizing of its forecast are that there won’t be any compensation for the decreasing revenues in Argentina, the short-term cost reduction that could impact clients won’t be executed, and, in general, the recalibration of the original assumptions.

It is always hard to lower expectations in a Fintech business where you are supposed to transform the financials of retail businesses. However, as an analyst, those are special moments where you could find opportunities. I think Fiserv’s CEO, Michael Lyons, has been brave to announce lower guidance, and this change in expectations is definitely a positive for Fiserv's investment thesis.

Besides this character, I like the company’s priority for the client and stopping tactical decisions designed to boost margin results at the expense of the customer. And I also appreciate the assets that the company has in the market, such as Clover, Commerce Hub, Finxact, STAR, Accel, Optis, and Vision Next. Those platforms and software are in a good position in the market, as we will see in the next session.

A promising market position

Despite those expectations, what lies beneath is a solid business in the market, and that is what we should lean on. The company is one of the largest companies in the industry at $5.2 billion in revenue (Figure 3), just behind Block’s revenue at $6.1 billion and way larger than the niche-specific Toast (TOST). That means the current customer base has trusted Fiserv, even if the last actions were non-customer-friendly. Besides, the company is aiming to push growth. Clover has been introduced in large international markets as Brazil and Europe. And the company is moving up-market to reach big retailers with Commerce Hub.

Fiserv's gross margin is one of the highest in the market at 59%, better than Block’s 44% and Toast’s 26% (Figure 4). It is important to notice that it is a good base to leverage into a business that is becoming more subscription-based, with higher fixed costs. Fiserv has the economics to invest in platform development to stay competitive and reach a leadership position in a competitive fintech market.

As always, I use Cash margin, net income adjusted for non-cash items such as amortization and depreciation, stock-based compensation, and deferred income tax, offering a clearer view of operational cash generation than standard margin metrics, as the main margin indicator. In Figure 5, you can see that Cash margin has lowered from 32% to 25% in line with what Fiserv’s CEO has stated that they won’t “cut certain costs, improve margins in the short term, but are now limiting our ability to serve clients in a world-class way.” I consider the company can stay at those lower margins if they invest in product and experiences for their customers.

At the end, Fiserv is the company that better converts each revenue into free cash flow, with a 50% free cash flow-to-revenue compared to 23% in Block and 9% in Toast. This is a good baseline for a business whose management is focused on the customer, the main value driver for the future of a business.

Valuation

In my valuation methodology, I discount projected free cash flows at an appropriate WACC. I will estimate the value drivers in Figure 7 in my ten-year model.

For revenue, I estimate that the company is going to decrease by 6% in 2025 and it will start growing in 2026 at a modest 2%. Later, the company will increase the growth rate to 5% in 2030 and stay at this growth rate until 2025. I consider, in a conservative approximation, that the maximum growth rate that the company is going to reach is 5% and considering the year 2026 as a year to make all the adjustments necessary and a transition year.

I estimate a ratio of cash margin-to-revenue that decreases to 20% in 2027 after 2026 management makes changes in the operation, and they are not able to compete with a higher margin, especially due to higher research and development costs. I see no big changes in the net working capital and capital expenditure.

Cash flows will be discounted at 7.4% WACC because the beta is 1.2, which I have estimated, because the historical measurement is less relevant due to its new situation. The risk-free rate is 4.2%. Debt, as a percentage of market capitalization, is 30%, and I consider it to have no excess cash. The perpetual growth rate is set at 3%, which is slightly better aligned with the long-term GDP growth rate.

My value estimate is $112.1 per share (Figure 8), up 84.4% from its current stock price. As we have seen in Figure 8, Fiserv’s Forward P/E comes down to 7 from a high close to 25 at the beginning of the year. The market was anticipating problems with the company. The last reset in expectations, the multiple went down from 17 to 7.

Risks

The first risk Fiserv is facing is the capacity to execute in an industry where high-tech players are emerging and dominating the market, such as Block and Toast. I see difficulties in moving fast in the software world. First Data acquisition has posed large liabilities in the culture and capacities of the workforce. And platform integration is key for customer satisfaction and to compete face-to-face with Block and Toast. The main software, Clover, was growing at 15-20% historically, and in the last earnings release, management stated an 8% GPV growth rate.

Margin deterioration can extend further in the coming quarters due to this competitive environment if the company needs to incur more expenses in research and development. EBITDA margin was 45.8% in Q3 2024 and has been reduced to 36.8% in Q3 2025.

We have to consider two threats that are relevant to Fiserv’s operations. Argentina is still a source of high currency and inflation risk. Leverage, debt-to-capital, has increased from 41% in Q3 2022 to 55% in Q3 2025. I do not view this as critical at present because operating cash flows to interest expense is a manageable 4.3, but it warrants close monitoring.

Risks are controlled in my estimation. In very aggressive assumptions, the probability of getting a value greater than the current stock price is controlled. I modeled in my risk simulation software, Crystall Ball, a variation in revenue growth from +/-5% in 95% of the cases. This means that the company won’t grow at all in my model in the most pessimistic scenario. In the case of cash margin, this variation in 95% of the cases goes from -5 percentage points to 5 percentage points. This means that in the worst case, the company will decrease from 25% to 15% in 2027. With those inputs, the model gives me a probability of a value greater than the current stock price of 85.75%.

Conclusion

In my opinion, the stock is undervalued after management changed guidance in Q3 2025. Forward P/E has decreased to a cheap value of 7, and this situation is a typical “buy the dip” situation vs. “a value trap”. I am confident the company is going to fix the culture and processes to transform Fiserv into a competitive player in the fintech space. The company can take advantage of great assets and platforms and a good market position.

I value the company at $112.1 per share, an 84.4% premium over its current stock price and a probability to exceed the current stock price at 85.75%. I recommend buying the company.
